title: Suit Size Chart — No More Guessing When Shopping Online
excerpt: Find your perfect fit, every time
content:
What's the most headache-inducing part of buying a suit online? It's not choosing the color or the style — it's the sizing. The same size S fits perfectly at one store and looks like it's borrowed at another. And that's before we even get to the differences between Chinese, European, and Japanese sizing — one wrong move and you end up with a suit that's either way too big or way too small. Today I'm going to walk you through the entire suit sizing system from start to finish — from height and weight to chest measurement to brand-to-brand differences — so you'll never have to stress about size again.

Let's start with a basic size chart using height and weight as the main references. Height 165–170 cm, weight 55–65 kg → size S. Height 170–175 cm, weight 65–75 kg → size M. Height 175–180 cm, weight 75–85 kg → size L. Height 180–185 cm, weight 85–95 kg → size XL. This is a "population average" — it's reasonably accurate for people with standard proportions. But here's the thing about size charts: hardly anyone actually has "standard model proportions." So relying purely on height and weight gives you roughly 60% accuracy at best. A much more reliable single metric is chest measurement. A chest of 96–100 cm generally maps to S or M, 100–104 cm to M or L, and 104–108 cm to L or XL. Chest measurement is a far more precise sizing benchmark than height and weight.

## Why This Matters

There's one major difference between sizing for a sports suit and a traditional suit: traditional suits should fit perfectly, while sports suits should be bought one size down or in your exact fitted size. Why? Because sports suits are made with stretch fabric. If you size up, you completely nullify the advantage of that stretch — the suit just ends up looking loose and baggy. Plus, sports suits are inherently casual pieces — a snug or slightly trim fit looks far better than sizing up "for comfort." I've looked at countless customer review photos of sports suits bought online, and sizing up almost always produces a lousy result. Practical advice: if you normally wear M (170/88A) in traditional suits, try S (165/84A) for sports suits — odds are it'll fit. Of course, you still need to consider whether a particular brand runs large or small, but "size down for sports suits" is a pretty universal rule.

Beyond height, weight, and chest, there are two other critical measurements: waist and shoulder width. Waist determines how well the trousers fit; shoulder width determines the jacket's silhouette. A lot of people just check height and weight and hit "buy". Then the package arrives, they try it on, and either the shoulders are sagging (making them look like a kid wearing their dad's clothes) or the pants waist is so loose they have to cinch it tight with a belt. The right approach: measure your bare chest, bare waist, and shoulder width, then compare them against the store's size chart. Most size charts will list "garment chest" and "garment shoulder width." The garment chest should be 8–12 cm larger than your bare chest (that's the "ease"). The garment shoulder width should be close to your actual shoulder width — 1–2 cm larger is perfectly normal. If the garment shoulder width is more than 4 cm larger than yours, the shoulders will definitely sag on you.

![](/images/suit-buying-guide.jpg)

Sizing differences based on the brand's country of origin are a real trap, and plenty of people have fallen into it. Here's the nutshell: European brands run large, Japanese and Korean brands run small, and Chinese domestic brands sit somewhere in the middle. For example, if you're a perfect M in a Chinese brand, you might need S in a European brand and L or even XL in a Japanese brand. Why is that? European body types tend to be larger on average, while Japanese sizing is built around their national average physique — the difference in body types shows up directly in the sizing. One more nuanced detail: Italian suit brands generally have narrower shoulders and a higher waistline, which suits Asian body types quite well. British brands tend to have wider shoulders and a longer torso, making them a better fit for taller people. So when buying a suit, don't just look at the letter size — understand the brand's underlying silhouette logic. An Italian M and a British M can differ by 2–3 cm in shoulder width.

## Key Point One: Fit Over Price

The sizing labels on Taobao (and other Chinese e-commerce platforms) are the most confusing part. On the same product, one store might label it "M/L/XL," another uses "46/48/50" (European sizing), and another uses "170/88A" (Chinese national standard). Let me break down how these convert. In the Chinese system, "170" means 170 cm height, "88" means 88 cm chest, and "A" means a standard build. European 46 roughly equals Chinese 170/84A, 48 equals 170/88A, 50 equals 175/92A, and 52 equals 180/96A. If the store only uses S/M/L, it gets even more chaotic — some brands define S as 165, others as 170. The safest bet is to check the product's "size chart" section, which lists concrete numbers: chest, shoulder width, jacket length. Numbers like "Shoulder 44 cm, Chest 108 cm, Length 72 cm" — compare your own measurements against those and you'll be ten times more accurate than relying on letter codes.

One sizing factor that many people overlook: fabric stretch. Wool fabric has almost no stretch. If you buy it too big, it stays too big — you can't "stretch it out." So when trying on or comparing sizes for pure wool suits, you need to leave enough ease — garment chest should be at least 10 cm larger than your bare chest. But sports suits with spandex have real stretch, so the ease can be reduced to 6–8 cm. That gives you a fit that's both snug and comfortable thanks to the elastic fabric. If you're unsure, lean toward bigger rather than smaller — this is an iron law in the suit world. Shoulders are very difficult to take in, and jacket length is even harder. But the waist can be taken in, and sleeves can be shortened. There's far more room to make something smaller than to make it bigger. If you buy too small, you can't stretch the chest or shoulders. So if you're torn between two sizes, go up and have a tailor take in the waist and sleeves.

Return policies are another thing you absolutely need to check. Many online stores or livestream suit sellers don't offer "7-day no-questions-asked returns" — especially for "custom" or "special offer" items. Make sure you read the return conditions before placing an order. For your first purchase from a store, confirm these three things: does it support 7-day no-questions-asked returns, who covers return shipping, and is post-try-on return allowed (some sellers say "trying it on doesn't affect resale" but then won't accept returns once you've actually worn it). Buying a suit isn't like buying a T-shirt — you only have about a 50% chance of getting the right fit. So the return policy really matters. A "free trial before you pay" service is the best-case scenario, and many platforms are already adopting this model. If that's not available, at least choose the "return shipping insurance" option — it costs almost nothing and saves you from paying more for return shipping than you did for the suit itself.

## Key Point Two: Color Coordination

Here's a little-known hack that isn't really a secret but most people don't think to try: spend a few bucks on a soft tape measure, get your measurements, and then message a few online custom-suit brand customer service agents. Send them your chest, waist, shoulder width, height, and weight, and ask: "What size in your brand would work for me?" These customer service reps handle dozens or even hundreds of sizing inquiries every single day. They have enormous practical experience and can usually nail your size at a glance. Plus, they understand different brands' sizing systems, so their recommendations are far more accurate than guessing on your own. I use this trick before every new brand purchase — nine out of ten times, the size they recommend is spot on. Give it a try. It takes just a few minutes and saves you a ton of hassle with returns and exchanges. Get the size right, and your online suit buying success rate doubles.

![](/images/suit-color.jpg)

The highest level of suit-wearing isn't making people think your clothes are expensive — it's making them think you're a well-dressed person. A good fit, a clean shirt, well-coordinated colors — nail these basics and you've already surpassed most people. Master the fundamentals before you chase personality.

One easily overlooked detail in suit styling is seasonal fabric awareness. In spring and summer, wear light, breathable fabrics; in fall and winter, go with slightly heavier wool. It's not realistic to wear the same wool suit year-round — in summer you sweat more and the wool can deform. Preparing suits in different fabrics for different seasons is the only way to always look polished.

## Key Point Three: Fabric Selection

What most people agonize over isn't whether they can afford a suit — it's whether they'll actually wear it. If you just remember three basic principles — the three-color rule, prioritize fit, and match the occasion — you won't go wrong wearing a suit. Everything else is about slowly building your own sense of style and taste through practice.

A suit isn't a one-time investment. A good suit lasting for several years is perfectly normal, but it does need proper care. Regular dry cleaning, proper hanging, and timely repairs can extend a suit's life considerably. When you do the math, the annual cost of wearing it is actually quite low.

Building a content site is all about persistence. It's not about writing three or five articles and stopping — it's about consistent, steady output. Write a little every day, publish a few articles each week, accumulate dozens each month. Six months from now, look back at what you've written and you'll realize you've built a decent content library. Search traffic is the compound interest on that library.

## Practical Tips

![](/images/suit-styling.jpg)

Never underestimate the long-term value of one great piece of content. A carefully written article may get very little traffic in its first few months, but over time its rankings will gradually rise, and it will eventually become a stable source of traffic. The content site business model is essentially turning today's creative work into tomorrow's returns.

What readers hate most is fluff — articles that say nothing. Every article needs concrete steps, real data, and actionable advice. Don't write filler just to hit a word count. Every paragraph should provide value to the reader. If an article can solve a specific problem for a reader, that person will almost certainly become a loyal follower.
